naming_inspiration.description: First name honors his paternal grandfather Dr. Julius Yemans Dewey (1801-1877), the Vermont physician and National Life Insurance Company founder.
First name honors his paternal grandfather Dr. Julius Yemans Dewey (1801-1877), the Vermont physician and National Life Insurance Company founder.

residence.note: 120 State Street; built new home 1889 on family site, moved old house to 144 State Street.

sources.label: Vermont Historical Society — Edward Dewey 8th Vermont Papers (1864)
sources.note: Quartermaster invoices and inventories from one year of regiment service.

narrative_blocks.body: In August 1856 Edward married Susan Griggs Lilley — the daughter of his father Julius's third wife Susan Elizabeth Griggs Lilley (married 1855). Edward and Susan had been stepsiblings-by-marriage for one year before becoming husband and wife. They had six children, four documented in this tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Quartermaster at Cedar Creek and Opequon
narrative_blocks.body: Edward joined the 8th Vermont Volunteer Infantry as Lieutenant Quartermaster in Louisiana January 1864, accompanied the regiment to Virginia in July, and saw action at Opequon and Cedar Creek. He was promoted Captain in February 1865, resigned three months later, and returned home. His quartermaster invoices for 1864 survive at the Vermont Historical Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Built the 1889 Queen Anne State Street mansion
narrative_blocks.body: Edward commissioned a Queen Anne brick house at 120 State Street Montpelier in 1889 — designed by city mayor and architect George Guernsey on the site of his family's earlier home. The old house was moved to 144 State Street where it stood until 1969. Edward's 1889 house was moved to 128 State Street in 1948 to make way for the Vermont State Office Building.

career.notes: Prepared for college but chose mercantile life until 1860.
Four-year tenure before Civil War service.
Returned from Civil War and joined NLI under his father's presidency. Elected vice-president June 1877; held position to January 1897.

military_service.notes: Joined 8th VT Volunteers as Quartermaster in Louisiana January 1864; accompanied regiment to Virginia July 1864; participated in battles of Opequon and Cedar Creek. Promoted Captain and Assistant Quartermaster, U.S. volunteers staff department February 11, 1865; resigned three months later. Quartermaster papers preserved at Vermont Historical Society.

institutions.institution_blurb: Civil War quartermaster who returned to serve as assistant secretary then vice-president of his father Julius's National Life Insurance Company until 1897.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built the 1889 Queen Anne brick house at 120 State Street Montpelier (designed by mayor George Guernsey) on the site of his family home.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Lilley (no further detail). Not built.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Susan Elizabeth Griggs (1816-1886) later became third wife of Edward's father Julius Yemans Dewey March 9, 1855 Montpelier VT — making Edward and Susan stepsiblings-by-marriage one year before they themselves married 1856. Not built.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT — Latitude 44.257843, Longitude -72.5944519.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Capt. Simeon Dewey (1770-1863), born Hebron CT, settled Berlin VT four miles from Montpelier, lived to age 93. m. Prudence Yemans (1772-1844) on February 27, 1794. Not built — non-tree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Prudence Yemans Dewey (1772-1844). Not built — non-tree.

sources.label: Vermont Historical Society — Dr. Julius Yemans Dewey portrait

notable.notable_blurb: Vermont physician; founded National Life Insurance

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Vermont's largest life insurance company
narrative_blocks.body: Julius founded the National Life Insurance Company at Montpelier in 1850 and served as its president until his death twenty-seven years later. He personally delivered the remittance for the company's first claim, prompting a public thank-you from the surviving family. NLI remains one of the ten oldest insurance companies in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Married three Susans and one Mary Perrin
narrative_blocks.body: After his Berlin sweetheart Mary Polly Perrin died in 1843 leaving four young children, Julius married twice more — both wives named Susan, neither bore him children. His third wife Susan Elizabeth Griggs Lilley had a daughter from a prior marriage who later married Julius's own son Edward — making Edward and his stepsister-in-law husband and wife.

narrative_blocks.header: Backed his son's beating from Pangborn the teacher
narrative_blocks.body: When eleven-year-old George Dewey led an attack on his Montpelier schoolteacher Z.K. Pangborn — and Pangborn flogged the boys into submission with a stick of stove fuel and walked the bruised George home to explain — Julius listened gravely, then thanked the teacher and asked him to repeat the punishment whenever necessary. George later credited the discipline with saving him from prison.

education.school_name: American Literary Scientific and Military Academy (Norwich Military School)
education.notes: Norwich Military School (later Norwich University) attended pre-medical.

career.notes: Practiced as 'most prominent practitioner and one of the leading citizens of the capital of Vermont' until founding NLI.
Founded 1850 (some sources 1848). Personally delivered the company's first claim remittance, prompting public thank-you from surviving family. Served as president until his death.
Documented founder of Christ Episcopal of Montpelier.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founder and president of the National Life Insurance Company of Montpelier — Vermont's largest life insurer.
Trustee of Norwich University; previously attended its predecessor American Literary Scientific and Military Academy.

artworks.blurb: Vermont Historical Society oil portrait c.1860, gift of Susan Howland estate.

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT — alongside Julius.

narrative_blocks.header: Burned the log cabin where she was born
narrative_blocks.body: Per family lore, Mary Polly was 'ashamed of having been born in a log cabin' — she and a sister later went up one night and burned the original Perrin cabin to the ground. Her father Zachariah's permanent farm at Scott Hill in Berlin Vermont stood on the same land, but the dirt-floor structure of her birth was gone by her own hand.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Manila Bay's Admiral, died at forty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Polly bore Admiral George Dewey on December 26, 1837 — her third child. He was five years old when she died at age 44 in September 1843 Montpelier Vermont. Her husband Julius later married twice more, but Mary Polly was the mother of all his children.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her elder sister Mary Perrin (1781-1798), born Hebron CT, who died at age seventeen — four months before her parents named the second baby Mary in her memory. The second Mary was sometimes called Polly to distinguish from her dead namesake.
